title: Cold Start & Warmup Sequence Specification for Inference Nodes
preview:
Purpose
This page defines the standardized cold start and warmup sequence for Redwood inference nodes (hosted / dedicated / private) to ensure predictable latency, controlled cost, and reproducible behavior across deployments. It describes the desired state transitions, required telemetry, configuration knobs, and an executable warmup recipe for common model classes.

Scope
- Applies to all GPU-backed inference nodes managed by node-provisioner and serve-runtime across Hosted API, Dedicated pools, and Private runtime-sidecars.
- Covers node lifecycle states: PROVISIONING -> BOOTSTRAPPING -> MODEL_PREFETCH -> KV_CACHE_WARM -> READY -> DEGRADED.
- Excludes application-level client warmup (client libraries should implement separate jitter/backoff).

Goals and SLOs
- Node readiness p95 <= 30s for warm reboots (same model pinned)
- Cold provisioning p95 <= 120s for full model load (large foundation models up to 70B in sharded mode)
- Model load time per-shard p95 <= 20s (target for shard sizes <= 8GB)
- KV cache priming success rate >= 99% within warmup window
- No more than 0.5% additional request error budget consumed by cold-starts in Normal load profiles

Key Concepts and Components
- node-provisioner: allocates VM/GPU nodes and performs OS-level bootstrapping (networks, mounts). Responsible for PROVISIONING -> BOOTSTRAPPING.
- runtime-agent (on-node): lightweight agent that coordinates model fetch, integrity verification, and exposes readiness via /internal/health/node-ready.
- model-cache: local disk/LV store where model shards are persisted and validated; sealed artifacts are signed and verified against artifact-provenance metadata.
- model-loader (integration in serve-runtime): loads model into GPU memory, shards KV cache if applicable, and reports model_load_time metric.
- kv-cache-service: optional prefilling of KV cache from persisted previous sessions or precomputed prefixes.

Acceptable Degradation Modes
1) Fallback to quantized variant (Q4_K_M) when model load time > configured threshold (see Config table).
2) Serve from smaller context window with early-exit policy for streaming requests until KV cache is warm.
3) Route low-priority requests to Best-Effort pool until node reaches READY.

Warmup Sequence (detailed)
1) PROVISIONING (node-provisioner)
   - Allocate requested GPU type and attach model-store volume
   - Emit metric: node_provision_requested{gpu_type} and node_provision_duration_seconds
2) BOOTSTRAPPING (runtime-agent)
   - Start runtime-agent, mount model-store, apply node-level flags from config service
   - Verify connectivity to model-catalog and key-management service (KMS)
   - Health probe: runtime-agent exposes /internal/health/agent-ready
3) MODEL_PREFETCH (model-loader)
   - Validate model artifact signature and compare checksum
   - Stream shards from model-store to local cache in parallel (default parallelism = min(4, vCPU_count))
   - Metric to emit: model_shard_stream_time_seconds and model_shards_failed
4) MODEL_LOAD (serve-runtime)
   - Load shards into device memory; allocate KV cache buffers according to flavor profile
   - Emit model_loaded{model,variant,shard_count} and model_load_time_seconds
5) KV_CACHE_WARM (kv-cache-service + serve-runtime)
   - Execute a configurable set of synthetic prompts to prime common attention patterns and populate KV entries
   - Default warmup prompt set: 10 prompts per shard tuned for tokens distribution representative of production workload
   - Metric: kv_cache_fill_ratio and kv_cache_priming_success
6) READY
   - runtime-agent sets /internal/health/node-ready = 200 once model_loaded and kv_cache_fill_ratio >= configured threshold (default 0.6)
   - Emit node_ready_time_seconds (from provision request to READY)

Configuration Table (required fields and suggested defaults)
| key | service | default | description |
| node.warmup.parallel_shard_streams | node-provisioner | 4 | Parallel streams used when fetching shards from model-store |
| node.warmup.kv_prompt_set | serve-runtime | /etc/warmup/default_prompts.txt | Path to warmup prompts used to seed KV cache |
| node.warmup.kv_threshold | serve-runtime | 0.6 | Fraction of expected KV entries that must be present before READY |
| node.warmup.model_load_timeout_s | runtime-agent | 120 | Max time to attempt model load before falling back to quantized variant |
| node.warmup.allow_quant_fallback | serve-runtime | true | Enable automatic fallback to quantized variant on load/timeouts |
| node.warmup.prefetch_retries | node-provisioner | 2 | Number of retries for shard streaming before failing node provisioning |

Warmup Prompt Example (default_prompts.txt)
- "Translate the following sentence to French: 'The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.'"
- "Summarize the key points: (a) product launch (b) target audience (c) go-to-market channels."
- "Classify the sentiment for: 'This update significantly improved our onboarding flow.'"

YAML Recipe: prefetch-and-prime (runtime-agent CLI)
---
steps:
  - name: prefetch-shards
    command: "/usr/local/bin/runtime-agent prefetch --model-id {{MODEL_ID}} --parallel 4 --dest /var/lib/model-cache"
    timeout: 90
  - name: load-model
    command: "/usr/local/bin/model-loader load --path /var/lib/model-cache/{{MODEL_ID}} --variant auto"
    timeout: 120
  - name: prime-kv
    command: "/usr/local/bin/kv-primer --prompts /etc/warmup/default_prompts.txt --concurrency 8 --budget-ms 5000"
    timeout: 30
---

Telemetry and Metrics (required)
- node_ready_time_seconds (histogram) - labels: region, gpu_type, pool_id, model_family
- model_load_time_seconds (histogram) - labels: model_id, variant, shard_index
- model_shard_stream_time_seconds (histogram) - labels: model_id, src_bucket
- kv_cache_fill_ratio (gauge) - labels: model_id, shard_group
- cold_request_latency_inflation_percent (gauge) - measure of latency delta for initial requests vs steady-state

Alerting and Runbook Integrations
- Alert: NodeWarmupFailure (SEV3) when node_ready_time_seconds.p95 > configured cold threshold (default 180s) or model_shards_failed > 0 for > 3 nodes in 15m
- Alert: KVPrimingLag (SEV4) when kv_cache_fill_ratio < node.warmup.kv_threshold for > 10m after model_loaded
- Runbook: See /confluence/eng-infra/runbooks/node-warmup-and-prewarm-troubleshooting (includes commands: runtime-agent logs, model-loader --debug, re-trigger prefetch)

Fallback and Safety Policies
- If model_load_time > node.warmup.model_load_timeout_s and allow_quant_fallback = true, automatically switch to quantized variant and mark node with label fallback_quant=true. Teams must record quality impact in rollout notes.
- Respect cost-control flag node.warmup.disable_prefetch in burst mode to avoid cross-tenant egress spikes.

Rollout and Adoption Guidance
1) Canary: enable warmup recipe for 1% of newly provisioned nodes in a single region for 48 hours and track node_ready_time_seconds and cold_request_latency_inflation_percent.
2) Gradual ramp: increase to 10% for 72 hours, then 50% for 7 days, validating no more than 2x baseline egress cost and no SLA regressions.
3) Full rollout: enable by default once metrics and cost are within acceptable ranges.

Ownership and Responsibilities
- eng-infra: node-provisioner and model-store maintenance, capacity accounting, and billing impact evaluation.
- eng-serving-runtime: runtime-agent, model-loader, KV primer, and readiness contract.
- product: communicate behavioral changes for Dedicated/Private customers and provide migration notes.

Open Questions and Future Work
- Adaptive warmup: investigate ML-driven prompt selection for KV priming to minimize tokens while maximizing hit-rate.
- Cross-tenant warmup sharing for identical model variants on the same node while preserving tenancy isolation and accounting.
- Persistence optimizations for incremental shard diffs to reduce prefetch bandwidth for minor patch releases.

References
- /confluence/architecture-and-standards/model-serving-standards/model-artifact-provenance-and-signing-standard-2026
- /confluence/architecture-and-standards/infra-standards/graceful-gpu-preemption-and-prewarming-guidelines-2026
- /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/sequence-length-aware-kernel-selection-guidelines-2026

Appendix: Troubleshooting checklist (quick)
1) Verify runtime-agent logs: sudo journalctl -u runtime-agent -n 500
2) Check model-store connectivity: curl -sSf https://model-catalog.internal/v1/models/{{MODEL_ID}}/manifest
3) Re-run prefetch: /usr/local/bin/runtime-agent prefetch --model-id {{MODEL_ID}} --parallel 2 --dest /var/lib/model-cache
4) If quant-fallback triggered, compare sample outputs against golden set and file a regression ticket with eng-serving-runtime

Document history
- 2024-11-15: Draft created by Priya Rao
- 2024-12-02: Added YAML recipe and updated metrics (review by Diego Martin)
- 2025-02-10: Finalized SLOs and rollout plan, published
text:
Purpose
This page defines the standardized cold start and warmup sequence for Redwood inference nodes (hosted / dedicated / private) to ensure predictable latency, controlled cost, and reproducible behavior across deployments. It describes the desired state transitions, required telemetry, configuration knobs, and an executable warmup recipe for common model classes.

Scope
- Applies to all GPU-backed inference nodes managed by node-provisioner and serve-runtime across Hosted API, Dedicated pools, and Private runtime-sidecars.
- Covers node lifecycle states: PROVISIONING -> BOOTSTRAPPING -> MODEL_PREFETCH -> KV_CACHE_WARM -> READY -> DEGRADED.
- Excludes application-level client warmup (client libraries should implement separate jitter/backoff).

Goals and SLOs
- Node readiness p95 <= 30s for warm reboots (same model pinned)
- Cold provisioning p95 <= 120s for full model load (large foundation models up to 70B in sharded mode)
- Model load time per-shard p95 <= 20s (target for shard sizes <= 8GB)
- KV cache priming success rate >= 99% within warmup window
- No more than 0.5% additional request error budget consumed by cold-starts in Normal load profiles

Key Concepts and Components
- node-provisioner: allocates VM/GPU nodes and performs OS-level bootstrapping (networks, mounts). Responsible for PROVISIONING -> BOOTSTRAPPING.
- runtime-agent (on-node): lightweight agent that coordinates model fetch, integrity verification, and exposes readiness via /internal/health/node-ready.
- model-cache: local disk/LV store where model shards are persisted and validated; sealed artifacts are signed and verified against artifact-provenance metadata.
- model-loader (integration in serve-runtime): loads model into GPU memory, shards KV cache if applicable, and reports model_load_time metric.
- kv-cache-service: optional prefilling of KV cache from persisted previous sessions or precomputed prefixes.

Acceptable Degradation Modes
1) Fallback to quantized variant (Q4_K_M) when model load time > configured threshold (see Config table).
2) Serve from smaller context window with early-exit policy for streaming requests until KV cache is warm.
3) Route low-priority requests to Best-Effort pool until node reaches READY.

Warmup Sequence (detailed)
1) PROVISIONING (node-provisioner)
   - Allocate requested GPU type and attach model-store volume
   - Emit metric: node_provision_requested{gpu_type} and node_provision_duration_seconds
2) BOOTSTRAPPING (runtime-agent)
   - Start runtime-agent, mount model-store, apply node-level flags from config service
   - Verify connectivity to model-catalog and key-management service (KMS)
   - Health probe: runtime-agent exposes /internal/health/agent-ready
3) MODEL_PREFETCH (model-loader)
   - Validate model artifact signature and compare checksum
   - Stream shards from model-store to local cache in parallel (default parallelism = min(4, vCPU_count))
   - Metric to emit: model_shard_stream_time_seconds and model_shards_failed
4) MODEL_LOAD (serve-runtime)
   - Load shards into device memory; allocate KV cache buffers according to flavor profile
   - Emit model_loaded{model,variant,shard_count} and model_load_time_seconds
5) KV_CACHE_WARM (kv-cache-service + serve-runtime)
   - Execute a configurable set of synthetic prompts to prime common attention patterns and populate KV entries
   - Default warmup prompt set: 10 prompts per shard tuned for tokens distribution representative of production workload
   - Metric: kv_cache_fill_ratio and kv_cache_priming_success
6) READY
   - runtime-agent sets /internal/health/node-ready = 200 once model_loaded and kv_cache_fill_ratio >= configured threshold (default 0.6)
   - Emit node_ready_time_seconds (from provision request to READY)

Configuration Table (required fields and suggested defaults)
| key | service | default | description |
| node.warmup.parallel_shard_streams | node-provisioner | 4 | Parallel streams used when fetching shards from model-store |
| node.warmup.kv_prompt_set | serve-runtime | /etc/warmup/default_prompts.txt | Path to warmup prompts used to seed KV cache |
| node.warmup.kv_threshold | serve-runtime | 0.6 | Fraction of expected KV entries that must be present before READY |
| node.warmup.model_load_timeout_s | runtime-agent | 120 | Max time to attempt model load before falling back to quantized variant |
| node.warmup.allow_quant_fallback | serve-runtime | true | Enable automatic fallback to quantized variant on load/timeouts |
| node.warmup.prefetch_retries | node-provisioner | 2 | Number of retries for shard streaming before failing node provisioning |

Warmup Prompt Example (default_prompts.txt)
- "Translate the following sentence to French: 'The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.'"
- "Summarize the key points: (a) product launch (b) target audience (c) go-to-market channels."
- "Classify the sentiment for: 'This update significantly improved our onboarding flow.'"

YAML Recipe: prefetch-and-prime (runtime-agent CLI)
---
steps:
  - name: prefetch-shards
    command: "/usr/local/bin/runtime-agent prefetch --model-id {{MODEL_ID}} --parallel 4 --dest /var/lib/model-cache"
    timeout: 90
  - name: load-model
    command: "/usr/local/bin/model-loader load --path /var/lib/model-cache/{{MODEL_ID}} --variant auto"
    timeout: 120
  - name: prime-kv
    command: "/usr/local/bin/kv-primer --prompts /etc/warmup/default_prompts.txt --concurrency 8 --budget-ms 5000"
    timeout: 30
---

Telemetry and Metrics (required)
- node_ready_time_seconds (histogram) - labels: region, gpu_type, pool_id, model_family
- model_load_time_seconds (histogram) - labels: model_id, variant, shard_index
- model_shard_stream_time_seconds (histogram) - labels: model_id, src_bucket
- kv_cache_fill_ratio (gauge) - labels: model_id, shard_group
- cold_request_latency_inflation_percent (gauge) - measure of latency delta for initial requests vs steady-state

Alerting and Runbook Integrations
- Alert: NodeWarmupFailure (SEV3) when node_ready_time_seconds.p95 > configured cold threshold (default 180s) or model_shards_failed > 0 for > 3 nodes in 15m
- Alert: KVPrimingLag (SEV4) when kv_cache_fill_ratio < node.warmup.kv_threshold for > 10m after model_loaded
- Runbook: See /confluence/eng-infra/runbooks/node-warmup-and-prewarm-troubleshooting (includes commands: runtime-agent logs, model-loader --debug, re-trigger prefetch)

Fallback and Safety Policies
- If model_load_time > node.warmup.model_load_timeout_s and allow_quant_fallback = true, automatically switch to quantized variant and mark node with label fallback_quant=true. Teams must record quality impact in rollout notes.
- Respect cost-control flag node.warmup.disable_prefetch in burst mode to avoid cross-tenant egress spikes.

Rollout and Adoption Guidance
1) Canary: enable warmup recipe for 1% of newly provisioned nodes in a single region for 48 hours and track node_ready_time_seconds and cold_request_latency_inflation_percent.
2) Gradual ramp: increase to 10% for 72 hours, then 50% for 7 days, validating no more than 2x baseline egress cost and no SLA regressions.
3) Full rollout: enable by default once metrics and cost are within acceptable ranges.

Ownership and Responsibilities
- eng-infra: node-provisioner and model-store maintenance, capacity accounting, and billing impact evaluation.
- eng-serving-runtime: runtime-agent, model-loader, KV primer, and readiness contract.
- product: communicate behavioral changes for Dedicated/Private customers and provide migration notes.

Open Questions and Future Work
- Adaptive warmup: investigate ML-driven prompt selection for KV priming to minimize tokens while maximizing hit-rate.
- Cross-tenant warmup sharing for identical model variants on the same node while preserving tenancy isolation and accounting.
- Persistence optimizations for incremental shard diffs to reduce prefetch bandwidth for minor patch releases.

References
- /confluence/architecture-and-standards/model-serving-standards/model-artifact-provenance-and-signing-standard-2026
- /confluence/architecture-and-standards/infra-standards/graceful-gpu-preemption-and-prewarming-guidelines-2026
- /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/sequence-length-aware-kernel-selection-guidelines-2026

Appendix: Troubleshooting checklist (quick)
1) Verify runtime-agent logs: sudo journalctl -u runtime-agent -n 500
2) Check model-store connectivity: curl -sSf https://model-catalog.internal/v1/models/{{MODEL_ID}}/manifest
3) Re-run prefetch: /usr/local/bin/runtime-agent prefetch --model-id {{MODEL_ID}} --parallel 2 --dest /var/lib/model-cache
4) If quant-fallback triggered, compare sample outputs against golden set and file a regression ticket with eng-serving-runtime

title: ADR-Template Scaffolding Distribution: CLI vs GitHub Template Repos
preview:
## Summary
We will ship **both** (a) GitHub template repositories as the source of truth and (b) a **minimal scaffolding CLI** as the recommended onboarding path.

The CLI is intentionally narrow in scope for v1:
- `init`: scaffold a template into a new directory and write a version stamp
- `doctor`: validate local environment and basic connectivity (API key present, model configured, collector reachable)

We will **not** ship a full lifecycle “app manager” in v1 (no `deploy`, no `upgrade in-place`, no automated migrations). Users can always fall back to GitHub template repos directly.

## Context
The DevEx org is building production-minded Quickstart Templates (RAG + agents) across Python and TypeScript, with standardized safe defaults and OpenTelemetry instrumentation.

Distribution is a critical part of “time-to-first-success”:
- We need a low-friction path from docs to a working app.
- We need reliable versioning so the docs and template behavior match.
- We need guardrails so customers do not accidentally leak secrets, emit high-cardinality telemetry, or ship insecure defaults.

Two distribution patterns were considered:
1) **GitHub template repos only** (users click “Use this template” or `git clone`)
2) A dedicated **scaffolding CLI** (users run a command to generate the project)

## Decision
**Adopt a hybrid approach:**
- **GitHub template repositories remain the canonical source** (code, README, CI, release tags).
- A **small CLI provides a smoother onboarding path** and ensures consistent project initialization and environment validation.

### What the CLI does (v1)
- `init`
  - Prompts for template selection (e.g., `rag-agent-python`, `rag-agent-typescript`)
  - Creates a project directory from the template contents
  - Writes a **template version stamp** (e.g., `redwood.template_version`) to enable support/debuggability
  - Optionally generates a `.env.example` and a local `.env` (without secrets)
- `doctor`
  - Checks required env vars are present (without printing values)
  - Performs a lightweight API connectivity check
  - Validates local OTel collector endpoint accessibility (when configured)
  - Prints actionable remediation steps and links to troubleshooting docs

### What the CLI explicitly does NOT do (v1)
- No `upgrade` that mutates an existing project
- No `deploy` command or cloud-provider integrations
- No automatic secret management; we will link to recommended approaches
- No background telemetry emission from the CLI itself (any template traffic tagging remains **opt-in** in generated app code)

## Options considered
### Option A — GitHub template repos only
**Description:** Users start via “Use this template” in GitHub, `git clone`, or copy/paste from docs.

**Pros**
- Lowest ongoing maintenance (no CLI packaging, distribution, install issues)
- Works in restricted environments where installing CLIs is hard
- Clear provenance (Git history, tags) and simple contribution model
- Easier for customers to fork and customize

**Cons**
- Higher onboarding variance (different clone paths, missing steps)
- Harder to enforce consistent initialization (version stamp, env scaffolding)
- Harder to run “pre-flight checks” (OTel collector setup, API key presence)
- Users frequently end up with template code that doesn’t match docs if they copy from main branch

### Option B — Dedicated scaffolding CLI only
**Description:** Users must install/run a CLI; templates may be embedded or fetched dynamically.

**Pros**
- Best onboarding UX (guided prompts, validation, consistent output)
- Can centralize checks and best-practice enforcement

**Cons**
- Higher maintenance burden (package publishing, OS support, updates)
- Risk of CLI becoming a product surface we must support long-term
- More complicated versioning: CLI version vs template version
- Potential security concerns if CLI ever handles secrets improperly

### Option C — Hybrid (chosen)
**Description:** GitHub repos are canonical; CLI scaffolds from tagged template releases and adds “doctor” checks.

**Pros**
- Good UX without making CLI a single point of failure
- Canonical source remains simple, forkable, and reviewable
- Supports both “quick start” and “restricted/enterprise” environments
- Improves supportability via version stamps and standardized checks

**Cons**
- Still requires CLI packaging and basic support
- Requires clear versioning policy and docs discipline

## Rationale
We chose Hybrid to balance:
- **Developer experience:** reduce time-to-first-success with guided setup and preflight validation.
- **Maintenance:** keep the CLI minimal and avoid ongoing complexity (deploy/upgrade workflows).
- **Versioning and support:** template release tags remain the source of truth; CLI initializes a specific, supportable version.
- **Enterprise realities:** some users cannot install CLIs or access GitHub easily; they can still use repo archives or internal mirrors.

## Design notes
### Source of truth and versioning
- Templates live in the `redwood-quickstarts` repo(s) and are released with **tagged versions** (e.g., `rag-agent-python@1.2.0`).
- The CLI defaults to the **latest stable release** for each template, but supports `--template-version` to pin.
- The generated project includes a **version stamp file** used for support:
  - Example: `redwood.template_version` containing `template=rag-agent-python` and `version=1.2.0`
- Docs will link to “latest” by default, with guidance on pinning for production use.

### CLI distribution
- CLI is published as an **npm package** (primary path) to support cross-platform execution (macOS/Linux/Windows) and easy “no-install” usage via `npx`.
- Secondary installation methods may be added later (Homebrew, standalone binary) if demand warrants.

### Security and privacy
- The CLI must never print secret values.
- The CLI must not transmit user code or environment information to Redwood.
- Any template-origin traffic tagging remains **opt-in** and must avoid sensitive data in headers/attributes.
- Generated projects must follow dependency pinning and vulnerability scanning policies.

### Observability
- `doctor` validates the presence of required instrumentation configuration without emitting high-cardinality attributes.
- The CLI links to the standard observability reference (metrics, spans, log fields) for verification.

## Consequences
### Positive
- Faster and more reliable onboarding for self-serve users.
- Lower support burden from misconfigured environments (API keys, collector endpoints).
- Better debugging through version stamps (support can ask “what template + version?”).

### Negative / risks
- CLI packaging issues (proxy restrictions, locked-down machines) can block some users.
  - Mitigation: GitHub repo path remains fully supported; docs include both paths.
- Version drift between docs and templates if “latest” moves quickly.
  - Mitigation: docs reference stable releases for launch; docs owners update links as part of template release process.

## Implementation plan (v1)
1. Implement `init` and `doctor` (no other commands).
2. Add a template manifest mapping template IDs to release tags.
3. Ensure generated projects include:
   - `.env.example`
   - version stamp file
   - README with troubleshooting and operational hooks
4. Add CI for CLI:
   - smoke test `init` for each template
   - smoke test `doctor` output for missing env vars
5. Update docs IA to present:
   - “CLI path” (recommended)
   - “Repo path” (fallback / enterprise)

## Acceptance criteria
- A new user can run one command to scaffold a template and follow a README to first successful request.
- `doctor` identifies the top misconfigurations with actionable output (missing key, wrong base URL, collector unreachable).
- The support team can identify template provenance via version stamp.
- No secrets are logged or printed by the CLI.

## Open questions / follow-ups
- Do we need a future `upgrade` story (e.g., “generate a diff” instead of in-place mutation)? If yes, define scope in a separate ADR.
- Should we add a Homebrew tap or standalone binary once adoption proves value?
- How do we support air-gapped customers (Private/on-prem) with mirrored template artifacts?

## Appendix: Trade-off comparison table
| Dimension | GitHub templates only | CLI only | Hybrid (chosen) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Onboarding UX | Medium | High | High |
| Maintenance cost | Low | High | Medium |
| Versioning clarity | High | Medium | High |
| Works in locked-down envs | High | Medium/Low | High |
| Pre-flight validation | Low | High | High |
| Risk of scope creep | Low | High | Medium |

## Owners
- Decision owner: Connor O'Brien (Release/versioning)
- Implementation lead: Monica Patel (DevEx)
- Observability review: Allison Grant
- Security review: Marcus Lin
- Docs integration: Elliot Price

| Maintenance cost | Low | High | Medium |
| Versioning clarity | High | Medium | High |
| Works in locked-down envs | High | Medium/Low | High |
| Pre-flight validation | Low | High | High |
| Risk of scope creep | Low | High | Medium |

## Owners
- Decision owner: Connor O'Brien (Release/versioning)
- Implementation lead: Monica Patel (DevEx)
- Observability review: Allison Grant
- Security review: Marcus Lin
- Docs integration: Elliot Price

title: ADR-015: Centralized policy evaluation vs. embedded authorization evaluation (RBAC v2)
preview:
## Status
Published (supersedes informal guidance in early RBAC v2 design drafts).

## Decision
**We will evaluate authorization policies in-process (“embedded evaluation”) using a shared, versioned authorization library**, rather than requiring services to call a centralized online policy-evaluation service on the request path.

We will still use **centralized policy distribution and lifecycle management** (policy bundle repository + signed artifacts + rollout tooling), but **the allow/deny decision for a request will be computed locally** by the component enforcing authorization (API Gateway and/or the target service).

## Context
RBAC v2 requires deny-by-default enforcement with consistent semantics across:
- API Gateway (edge enforcement for Hosted API + Console)
- Control Plane services (org/project/env/deployment management)
- Dedicated + Private admin operations (including restricted environments)

We considered two architectural approaches for policy evaluation:
1) **Centralized online evaluation**: services call an internal “AuthZ Policy Service” to compute allow/deny.
2) **Embedded evaluation**: services run a shared evaluator library locally; policies are fetched/distributed out-of-band.

Key constraints and requirements:
- **Latency**: authorization must add negligible overhead. Target is <2 ms p95 added latency at Gateway and <1 ms p95 for service-local checks for typical requests.
- **Reliability**: authz must not introduce a new hard dependency that can take down traffic. Deny-by-default is required for security; availability must be addressed without widening implicit allow.
- **Multi-deployment**: Private deployments can be air-gapped or have constrained connectivity. Dedicated and Private customers also demand predictable failure modes.
- **Auditability**: we must log consistent decision context (subject/action/resource/scope/policy version/decision/denial reason).
- **Operational simplicity**: minimize new always-on services and on-call surface area, while enabling safe policy updates and fast rollback.

## Decision drivers
- Minimize request-path dependencies (avoid a new “authz” microservice that all other services must call).
- Support Private/on-prem deployments without requiring a highly available central service.
- Keep semantics consistent and testable (same evaluator code everywhere).
- Enable policy rollouts/rollbacks with clear versioning, validation, and visibility.
- Maintain strong security posture: deny-by-default, least privilege, and no “fail open” behavior.

## Considered options

### Option A — Centralized policy evaluation service (online)
**Description:** Every protected operation calls an internal policy evaluation endpoint (e.g., `POST /v1/evaluate`) with subject, action, resource scope, and context. The service returns allow/deny + reasons.

**Pros**
- Single decision point; easiest to change logic centrally.
- Simplifies clients (no policy parsing, fewer local caches).
- Centralized metrics and logging possible.

**Cons / risks**
- Introduces a new high-QPS dependency on the request path for all critical routes.
- Requires HA, multi-region routing, and strict SLOs; authz service becomes a Tier-0 system.
- Failure modes are hard: to preserve security we must deny on failure, which can become a global outage. To preserve availability we’d be pressured to fail open, which violates secure defaults.
- Challenging for Private deployments (air-gapped or restricted networking): would require bundling/running another always-on service and its HA plan.
- Potential for thundering-herd and cascading failures during incident conditions (authz QPS spikes during retries, partial outages, or latency regressions).

**Operational implications**
- New on-call rotation / escalation path.
- Needs dedicated capacity planning (p95, p99, tail latencies) and strict dependency management.
- Requires careful changes to avoid fleet-wide incidents.

### Option B — Embedded policy evaluation (in-process) with centralized distribution
**Description:** Gateway and services link a shared `authz-evaluator` library. Policies are distributed as signed, versioned bundles. Evaluation happens in-process using local caches and a local policy snapshot.

**Pros**
- Removes request-path dependency on a centralized service.
- Works naturally in Hosted, Dedicated, and Private environments.
- Predictable latency (CPU-only local computation; no network hop).
- Scales horizontally with existing services; avoids one global bottleneck.
- Enables deterministic behavior for incident response: if policies are available locally, traffic continues even during control-plane partial outages.

**Cons / risks**
- Requires robust policy distribution and versioning.
- Risk of policy drift if distribution is misconfigured; must track and surface policy versions in telemetry.
- Library upgrades must be managed across many services (though this is standard for Redwood shared libraries).
- Need consistent caching semantics and invalidation triggers across components.

**Operational implications**
- Fewer Tier-0 services; more emphasis on release discipline for shared libraries and policy artifacts.
- Requires strong CI validation and runtime diagnostics (e.g., “policy version loaded”, “cache hit rate”, denial reason codes).

## Decision details
We choose **Option B (embedded evaluation)**.

### Reference architecture (text diagram)
Request path (Hosted/Dedicated):

1. Client → **API Gateway**
2. Gateway authenticates subject (API key / session / SSO)
3. Gateway determines `action` + `resource_scope` (org/project/env/deployment/cluster)
4. Gateway calls embedded evaluator: `Evaluate(subject, action, scope, context)`
5. If allowed: forward to service; if denied: return standardized 403 with denial reason code
6. **Target service** performs a second check for service-owned sensitive operations (defense in depth) using the same library.

Policy path:
- Policy bundles are authored in `rbac-policy-bundle` repo, linted, signed, and released as versioned artifacts.
- Each environment (Hosted region, Dedicated cluster, Private install) runs a lightweight **Policy Bundle Distributor** job (or equivalent mechanism) that fetches artifacts, verifies signatures, and stores them in a local, read-optimized store.
- Services/Gateway load the current policy snapshot at startup and refresh on a controlled cadence (plus event-driven refresh where available).

### Enforcement boundaries
- **Gateway enforcement:** required for all external-facing endpoints. Deny-by-default applies when an endpoint is annotated as “requires permission X”.
- **Service enforcement:** required for control-plane mutations and any endpoint that can be invoked through internal paths or that affects security-relevant state (e.g., org/user/role bindings, routing policies, key rotation, audit export). This prevents bypass if a route is exposed internally or via misconfiguration.

### Caching and staleness
- Evaluator uses **scoped caching** keyed by:
  - subject principal ID + org ID
  - action
  - scope tuple (org/project/env/deployment/cluster)
  - policy bundle version
  - evaluator schema version
- Default cache TTL target: **30–60 seconds** for high-QPS endpoints, with immediate invalidation on:
  - role binding changes
  - role template updates
  - policy bundle version update

Rationale: small TTL reduces risk of stale allows after revocation while preserving latency goals.

### Failure modes
- **Policy snapshot missing/unloadable**: deny-by-default for protected operations; emit a high-severity alert (S1) and include `authz_error=POLICY_UNAVAILABLE` in decision logs.
- **Policy refresh failure**: continue using last-known-good snapshot; alert if refresh is stale beyond threshold (e.g., >30 minutes Hosted; configurable for Private).
- **Evaluator errors** (bug/exception): treat as deny for protected operations; include `authz_error=EVALUATOR_EXCEPTION` and capture stack fingerprint to error tracking.

### Audit logging impact
Embedded evaluation must still generate evidence-grade logs. All enforcement points (Gateway and services for sensitive mutations) must log:
- subject (principal type + id)
- action (permission string)
- resource scope (org/project/env/deployment/cluster)
- decision (allow/deny)
- denial reason code(s)
- policy bundle version + evaluator version
- request correlation identifiers (trace/span, request id)

This aligns with the audit log schema extension defined in `authz-decision-logging-schema`.

## Rationale
Embedded evaluation best satisfies Redwood’s core requirements:
- **Latency**: eliminates extra network hop and tail latency risks.
- **Reliability**: avoids turning a central authz service into a global choke point. Authorization remains available as long as the service instance is available.
- **Private deployments**: supports air-gapped and constrained environments with artifact-based policy distribution.
- **Security**: deny-by-default remains enforceable; “last-known-good” snapshot is acceptable when refresh fails, but “no policy loaded” is a deny condition.
- **Consistency**: a single shared library provides consistent semantics, denial reason codes, and audit fields.

## Consequences

### Positive
- Reduced request-path dependencies and improved blast-radius isolation.
- Consistent semantics across Gateway and services.
- Easier to support Dedicated/Private without running an additional always-on service.

### Negative / trade-offs
- Policy distribution and versioning become critical; we must invest in:
  - artifact signing and verification
  - bundle integrity checks
  - rollout tooling and safe rollback
  - clear visibility into “which policy version is running where”
- Shared library upgrades require coordination and release management.

### Required follow-ups
1. Implement/standardize a policy evaluator client library with safe caching and metrics (owner: Jordan Lee).
2. Add policy version reporting to health endpoints and telemetry dashboards (owner: Logan Wright).
3. Update API standards to require endpoint permission annotations and deny-by-default middleware/interceptors (owner: Amara Diallo / Ethan Park).
4. Ensure audit-log exporter maps authz decision fields for SIEM export (owner: Kevin Osei).

## Implementation notes (non-normative)
- Library API (illustrative):
  - `Evaluate(subject, permission, scope, context) -> {decision, denial_reasons, policy_version}`
  - `Explain(subject, permission, scope, context) -> debug trace` (admin-only; gated and rate-limited)
- Policy bundles should be immutable; updates are new versions.
- Services must treat policy bundle version as part of their “runtime config” and include it in incident reports.

## Security considerations
- No “fail open” behavior for protected actions.
- Debug/explain output must be admin-gated and avoid leaking sensitive policy details to unprivileged users.
- Policy bundle artifacts must be signed and verified in all deployment modes.

## Operational considerations
- Define SLOs for policy distribution freshness (Hosted vs Private configurable).
- Alerting:
  - `policy_snapshot_age_seconds` above threshold
  - `policy_load_failures` non-zero
  - spikes in `authz_denied_total` (with breakdown by denial reason)
  - evaluator latency p95/p99 regressions

## Alternatives not chosen
We explicitly did not choose an online centralized evaluator on the request path. If a centralized component is introduced later, it should be limited to:
- policy authoring/validation
- bundle signing/releasing
- out-of-band distribution and version orchestration

Any future online evaluator proposal must include a hard reliability plan that does not require fail-open semantics.

## Open questions
- For ultra-high-QPS inference endpoints, should we allow gateway-only enforcement for a subset of read-only actions to reduce duplicate checks? (Default remains defense-in-depth for sensitive mutations.)
- Do we need push-based invalidation for role changes in Hosted to reduce revocation latency below 30 seconds in certain enterprise SKUs?

## Changelog
- 2025-02-07: Clarified failure mode behavior (last-known-good vs missing policy snapshot) and required follow-ups for telemetry + SIEM mapping.
- 2025-01-22: Initial ADR drafted and circulated for review.

title: ADR-022: Public error contract for data residency policy violations
preview:
## Status
Published (effective for Hosted API and applicable Dedicated entrypoints).

## Summary
This ADR defines the **public-facing error contract** returned when a request is blocked by **Hosted data residency region pinning**. It standardizes:
- HTTP status code and response shape
- Stable machine-readable error codes
- Message templates and non-leaky guidance
- Request ID linkage for support correlation
- Streaming endpoint behavior
- SDK surface expectations (typed exception + fields)

This contract is used by:
- API Gateway enforcement (pre-routing)
- Smart Routing (as a defensive backstop; must not return divergent formats)
- SDKs (Python/TS/Go) mapping to a dedicated error type

## Context / Problem
We are introducing region pinning (allowed region set + primary region) for Hosted API. When a request would be processed outside the pinned region set (including via fallback), the platform must block the request deterministically and return a **clear and actionable** error.

Prior behavior in similar policy blocks has been inconsistent:
- Different endpoints returned different status codes (403/409/500)
- Streaming paths sometimes returned generic 500s after the connection was established
- Clients had difficulty correlating blocks with internal logs due to missing request IDs

We need a stable contract that:
1) is unambiguous for programmatic handling,
2) is consistent across streaming and non-streaming endpoints,
3) avoids exposing sensitive internal routing or infrastructure details,
4) provides enough context for customers to remediate without contacting support.

## Decision
### 1) HTTP status
For residency policy violations, the API MUST return:
- **HTTP 409 Conflict**

Rationale:
- The request is syntactically valid and authenticated, but conflicts with a configured policy state (residency constraints).
- 403 Forbidden conflates authorization vs. policy configuration and is commonly retried/handled incorrectly.
- 422 Unprocessable Entity is typically reserved for semantic payload validation; this is not a payload issue.

### 2) Canonical error envelope
All residency blocks MUST use the standard Redwood error envelope:

Response headers:
- `x-redwood-request-id: <req_...>` (always present)
- `content-type: application/json` (for non-streaming responses)

Response JSON body:
- top-level key: `error`
- `error.code` is stable and machine-readable
- `error.message` is human-readable and actionable
- `error.request_id` matches `x-redwood-request-id`
- `error.help_url` points to public documentation
- `error.details` provides bounded, non-sensitive diagnostic fields

### 3) Error codes
#### 3.1 Primary code (stable)
All residency blocks MUST set:
- `error.code = "residency_policy_violation"`

#### 3.2 Subcode (stable)
Additionally, the API MUST set:
- `error.subcode` to one of:
  - `"region_not_allowed"` (candidate region is outside allowed set)
  - `"primary_region_unavailable"` (primary region would have been used, but is unavailable and cross-region fallback is not allowed)
  - `"policy_misconfigured"` (invalid/empty allowed regions, invalid primary, or invalid precedence state that passed earlier validation)

Notes:
- `subcode` is intended for customer-side routing/UX and for stable metrics.
- Avoid adding endpoint-specific subcodes; keep this list short and documented.

### 4) Required fields
The following fields are REQUIRED in `error`:
- `message` (string)
- `code` (string)
- `subcode` (string)
- `request_id` (string)
- `help_url` (string)

The following fields are REQUIRED in `error.details`:
- `allowed_regions` (comma-separated string of region IDs; see “Region identifiers” below)
- `candidate_region` (string; the region the system would have selected without the block)
- `policy_scope` (string: `"org" | "project" | "api_key"`)

The following fields are OPTIONAL in `error.details`:
- `primary_region` (string)
- `policy_id` (opaque string identifier; safe to expose)

### 5) Region identifiers
Public region identifiers in error payloads MUST use the same region IDs customers see in Console and docs:
- `"us"`, `"eu"`, `"apac"` (tier-1)
- or more specific when the customer has configured it (e.g., `"eu-west"`), as documented.

Do not expose internal cloud region names or infrastructure locality identifiers in the public error payload.

### 6) Message templates
Messages MUST:
- State that the request was blocked by data residency policy
- Provide the allowed region(s)
- Provide a single remediation step (Console path and/or API setting)
- Avoid mentioning internal failover groups, capacity tiers, or provider-specific regions

Approved templates (gateway selects based on subcode):

**A) region_not_allowed**
"Request blocked by data residency policy. This project is restricted to regions: {allowed_regions}. This request would be processed in: {candidate_region}. Update your residency settings in the Redwood Console (Settings → Data residency) or use an API key for an allowed region. See: {help_url}."

**B) primary_region_unavailable**
"Request blocked by data residency policy. The primary region ({primary_region}) is temporarily unavailable and cross-region fallback is not permitted for this project. Retry later or contact support if this persists. See: {help_url}."

**C) policy_misconfigured**
"Request blocked by data residency policy due to a configuration error. Verify your allowed regions and primary region in the Redwood Console (Settings → Data residency). See: {help_url}."

### 7) Streaming endpoints behavior
Residency enforcement MUST occur **before any streaming bytes are written**.

For streaming endpoints (SSE / chunked responses):
- If the request is blocked, the server MUST return **HTTP 409** with the standard JSON error body (i.e., do not start the stream).

Non-compliant behavior (explicitly disallowed):
- returning HTTP 200 and then emitting an ad-hoc "error" SSE event
- returning a generic 500 mid-stream
- closing the connection without a structured error

### 8) Retry semantics
Clients/SDKs MUST treat `residency_policy_violation` as **non-retriable by default**.

Exception:
- `primary_region_unavailable` MAY be retried with backoff, but MUST NOT attempt cross-region reroute by default.

### 9) SDK surface requirements
SDKs MUST:
- Create a dedicated, typed exception (name by language):
  - Python: `ResidencyPolicyViolationError`
  - TypeScript: `ResidencyPolicyViolationError`
  - Go: `*ResidencyPolicyViolationError`
- Include fields/properties:
  - `code` (always `residency_policy_violation`)
  - `subcode`
  - `message`
  - `request_id`
  - `help_url`
  - `allowed_regions`
  - `candidate_region`
  - `primary_region` (optional)
  - `policy_scope`
  - `policy_id` (optional)
- Preserve the raw HTTP status (409) and headers for debugging.

SDKs SHOULD:
- Provide a short default suggestion in docs/examples: “Check Console → Settings → Data residency.”
- Avoid automatically switching endpoints/regions unless the customer explicitly opts in.

## Error payload examples
### Example 1: Region not allowed
HTTP/1.1 409 Conflict
x-redwood-request-id: req_01J3H7Y0Q3J2QF3B4A1Z9PZQ0N
content-type: application/json

{
  "error": {
    "message": "Request blocked by data residency policy. This project is restricted to regions: eu. This request would be processed in: us. Update your residency settings in the Redwood Console (Settings → Data residency) or use an API key for an allowed region. See: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/errors/residency-policy-violation.",
    "code": "residency_policy_violation",
    "subcode": "region_not_allowed",
    "request_id": "req_01J3H7Y0Q3J2QF3B4A1Z9PZQ0N",
    "help_url": "https://docs.redwoodinference.com/errors/residency-policy-violation",
    "details": {
      "allowed_regions": "eu",
      "candidate_region": "us",
      "primary_region": "eu",
      "policy_scope": "project",
      "policy_id": "pol_8f2b1f2e4f1a"
    }
  }
}

### Example 2: Primary region unavailable (no cross-region fallback)
HTTP/1.1 409 Conflict
x-redwood-request-id: req_01J3H82A4K0YB2G9D6Z7E0ZVQ9
content-type: application/json

{
  "error": {
    "message": "Request blocked by data residency policy. The primary region (eu) is temporarily unavailable and cross-region fallback is not permitted for this project. Retry later or contact support if this persists. See: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/errors/residency-policy-violation.",
    "code": "residency_policy_violation",
    "subcode": "primary_region_unavailable",
    "request_id": "req_01J3H82A4K0YB2G9D6Z7E0ZVQ9",
    "help_url": "https://docs.redwoodinference.com/errors/residency-policy-violation",
    "details": {
      "allowed_regions": "eu",
      "candidate_region": "us",
      "primary_region": "eu",
      "policy_scope": "org",
      "policy_id": "pol_ba31c7a9c102"
    }
  }
}

## Implementation notes (non-normative)
- Gateway is the enforcement source of truth for formatting. Downstream services may attach structured context for logs/metrics but MUST NOT craft customer-facing payloads.
- Routing may detect a violation as a backstop. If so, it must return the same envelope/fields (or forward a gateway-generated error).
- Ensure request ID generation happens early (before policy evaluation) so blocked requests are traceable.

## Security and privacy considerations
- Do not include prompts, completions, embeddings, or customer identifiers in the error payload.
- `policy_id` is safe to expose only as an opaque ID (no encoding of org/project identifiers).
- `candidate_region` must not reveal internal provider region codes.
- `help_url` must point to public docs that do not reveal internal architecture.

## Consequences
### Benefits
- Consistent customer experience across endpoints
- Deterministic handling for streaming clients (no mid-stream 500)
- Clear remediation reduces support load
- Strong correlation via request ID

### Trade-offs
- 409 may surprise some customers expecting 403; mitigated by docs and SDK typing.
- Includes a small amount of diagnostic region info (allowed/candidate). This is intentional and bounded.

## Rollout / Migration
1) Implement contract in gateway (feature-flagged).
2) Update SDKs to surface typed exception and fields.
3) Update public docs and error catalog entry for `residency_policy_violation`.
4) Add conformance tests:
   - non-streaming block returns 409 + envelope
   - streaming block returns 409 pre-stream
   - request_id header/body match
5) After rollout, treat deviations (500s, missing fields) as P1 bug for residency-enabled orgs.

## Open questions (resolved)
- Expose policy identifier? **Yes**, as `policy_id` (opaque), optional.
- Which status code? **409 Conflict**.
- Streaming semantics? **Block pre-stream; return normal JSON error**.
text:
## Status
Published (effective for Hosted API and applicable Dedicated entrypoints).

## Summary
This ADR defines the **public-facing error contract** returned when a request is blocked by **Hosted data residency region pinning**. It standardizes:
- HTTP status code and response shape
- Stable machine-readable error codes
- Message templates and non-leaky guidance
- Request ID linkage for support correlation
- Streaming endpoint behavior
- SDK surface expectations (typed exception + fields)

This contract is used by:
- API Gateway enforcement (pre-routing)
- Smart Routing (as a defensive backstop; must not return divergent formats)
- SDKs (Python/TS/Go) mapping to a dedicated error type

## Context / Problem
We are introducing region pinning (allowed region set + primary region) for Hosted API. When a request would be processed outside the pinned region set (including via fallback), the platform must block the request deterministically and return a **clear and actionable** error.

Prior behavior in similar policy blocks has been inconsistent:
- Different endpoints returned different status codes (403/409/500)
- Streaming paths sometimes returned generic 500s after the connection was established
- Clients had difficulty correlating blocks with internal logs due to missing request IDs

We need a stable contract that:
1) is unambiguous for programmatic handling,
2) is consistent across streaming and non-streaming endpoints,
3) avoids exposing sensitive internal routing or infrastructure details,
4) provides enough context for customers to remediate without contacting support.

## Decision
### 1) HTTP status
For residency policy violations, the API MUST return:
- **HTTP 409 Conflict**

Rationale:
- The request is syntactically valid and authenticated, but conflicts with a configured policy state (residency constraints).
- 403 Forbidden conflates authorization vs. policy configuration and is commonly retried/handled incorrectly.
- 422 Unprocessable Entity is typically reserved for semantic payload validation; this is not a payload issue.

### 2) Canonical error envelope
All residency blocks MUST use the standard Redwood error envelope:

Response headers:
- `x-redwood-request-id: <req_...>` (always present)
- `content-type: application/json` (for non-streaming responses)

Response JSON body:
- top-level key: `error`
- `error.code` is stable and machine-readable
- `error.message` is human-readable and actionable
- `error.request_id` matches `x-redwood-request-id`
- `error.help_url` points to public documentation
- `error.details` provides bounded, non-sensitive diagnostic fields

### 3) Error codes
#### 3.1 Primary code (stable)
All residency blocks MUST set:
- `error.code = "residency_policy_violation"`

#### 3.2 Subcode (stable)
Additionally, the API MUST set:
- `error.subcode` to one of:
  - `"region_not_allowed"` (candidate region is outside allowed set)
  - `"primary_region_unavailable"` (primary region would have been used, but is unavailable and cross-region fallback is not allowed)
  - `"policy_misconfigured"` (invalid/empty allowed regions, invalid primary, or invalid precedence state that passed earlier validation)

Notes:
- `subcode` is intended for customer-side routing/UX and for stable metrics.
- Avoid adding endpoint-specific subcodes; keep this list short and documented.

### 4) Required fields
The following fields are REQUIRED in `error`:
- `message` (string)
- `code` (string)
- `subcode` (string)
- `request_id` (string)
- `help_url` (string)

The following fields are REQUIRED in `error.details`:
- `allowed_regions` (comma-separated string of region IDs; see “Region identifiers” below)
- `candidate_region` (string; the region the system would have selected without the block)
- `policy_scope` (string: `"org" | "project" | "api_key"`)

The following fields are OPTIONAL in `error.details`:
- `primary_region` (string)
- `policy_id` (opaque string identifier; safe to expose)

### 5) Region identifiers
Public region identifiers in error payloads MUST use the same region IDs customers see in Console and docs:
- `"us"`, `"eu"`, `"apac"` (tier-1)
- or more specific when the customer has configured it (e.g., `"eu-west"`), as documented.

Do not expose internal cloud region names or infrastructure locality identifiers in the public error payload.

### 6) Message templates
Messages MUST:
- State that the request was blocked by data residency policy
- Provide the allowed region(s)
- Provide a single remediation step (Console path and/or API setting)
- Avoid mentioning internal failover groups, capacity tiers, or provider-specific regions

Approved templates (gateway selects based on subcode):

**A) region_not_allowed**
"Request blocked by data residency policy. This project is restricted to regions: {allowed_regions}. This request would be processed in: {candidate_region}. Update your residency settings in the Redwood Console (Settings → Data residency) or use an API key for an allowed region. See: {help_url}."

**B) primary_region_unavailable**
"Request blocked by data residency policy. The primary region ({primary_region}) is temporarily unavailable and cross-region fallback is not permitted for this project. Retry later or contact support if this persists. See: {help_url}."

**C) policy_misconfigured**
"Request blocked by data residency policy due to a configuration error. Verify your allowed regions and primary region in the Redwood Console (Settings → Data residency). See: {help_url}."

### 7) Streaming endpoints behavior
Residency enforcement MUST occur **before any streaming bytes are written**.

For streaming endpoints (SSE / chunked responses):
- If the request is blocked, the server MUST return **HTTP 409** with the standard JSON error body (i.e., do not start the stream).

Non-compliant behavior (explicitly disallowed):
- returning HTTP 200 and then emitting an ad-hoc "error" SSE event
- returning a generic 500 mid-stream
- closing the connection without a structured error

### 8) Retry semantics
Clients/SDKs MUST treat `residency_policy_violation` as **non-retriable by default**.

Exception:
- `primary_region_unavailable` MAY be retried with backoff, but MUST NOT attempt cross-region reroute by default.

### 9) SDK surface requirements
SDKs MUST:
- Create a dedicated, typed exception (name by language):
  - Python: `ResidencyPolicyViolationError`
  - TypeScript: `ResidencyPolicyViolationError`
  - Go: `*ResidencyPolicyViolationError`
- Include fields/properties:
  - `code` (always `residency_policy_violation`)
  - `subcode`
  - `message`
  - `request_id`
  - `help_url`
  - `allowed_regions`
  - `candidate_region`
  - `primary_region` (optional)
  - `policy_scope`
  - `policy_id` (optional)
- Preserve the raw HTTP status (409) and headers for debugging.

SDKs SHOULD:
- Provide a short default suggestion in docs/examples: “Check Console → Settings → Data residency.”
- Avoid automatically switching endpoints/regions unless the customer explicitly opts in.

## Error payload examples
### Example 1: Region not allowed
HTTP/1.1 409 Conflict
x-redwood-request-id: req_01J3H7Y0Q3J2QF3B4A1Z9PZQ0N
content-type: application/json

{
  "error": {
    "message": "Request blocked by data residency policy. This project is restricted to regions: eu. This request would be processed in: us. Update your residency settings in the Redwood Console (Settings → Data residency) or use an API key for an allowed region. See: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/errors/residency-policy-violation.",
    "code": "residency_policy_violation",
    "subcode": "region_not_allowed",
    "request_id": "req_01J3H7Y0Q3J2QF3B4A1Z9PZQ0N",
    "help_url": "https://docs.redwoodinference.com/errors/residency-policy-violation",
    "details": {
      "allowed_regions": "eu",
      "candidate_region": "us",
      "primary_region": "eu",
      "policy_scope": "project",
      "policy_id": "pol_8f2b1f2e4f1a"
    }
  }
}

### Example 2: Primary region unavailable (no cross-region fallback)
HTTP/1.1 409 Conflict
x-redwood-request-id: req_01J3H82A4K0YB2G9D6Z7E0ZVQ9
content-type: application/json

{
  "error": {
    "message": "Request blocked by data residency policy. The primary region (eu) is temporarily unavailable and cross-region fallback is not permitted for this project. Retry later or contact support if this persists. See: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/errors/residency-policy-violation.",
    "code": "residency_policy_violation",
    "subcode": "primary_region_unavailable",
    "request_id": "req_01J3H82A4K0YB2G9D6Z7E0ZVQ9",
    "help_url": "https://docs.redwoodinference.com/errors/residency-policy-violation",
    "details": {
      "allowed_regions": "eu",
      "candidate_region": "us",
      "primary_region": "eu",
      "policy_scope": "org",
      "policy_id": "pol_ba31c7a9c102"
    }
  }
}

## Implementation notes (non-normative)
- Gateway is the enforcement source of truth for formatting. Downstream services may attach structured context for logs/metrics but MUST NOT craft customer-facing payloads.
- Routing may detect a violation as a backstop. If so, it must return the same envelope/fields (or forward a gateway-generated error).
- Ensure request ID generation happens early (before policy evaluation) so blocked requests are traceable.

## Security and privacy considerations
- Do not include prompts, completions, embeddings, or customer identifiers in the error payload.
- `policy_id` is safe to expose only as an opaque ID (no encoding of org/project identifiers).
- `candidate_region` must not reveal internal provider region codes.
- `help_url` must point to public docs that do not reveal internal architecture.

## Consequences
### Benefits
- Consistent customer experience across endpoints
- Deterministic handling for streaming clients (no mid-stream 500)
- Clear remediation reduces support load
- Strong correlation via request ID

### Trade-offs
- 409 may surprise some customers expecting 403; mitigated by docs and SDK typing.
- Includes a small amount of diagnostic region info (allowed/candidate). This is intentional and bounded.

## Rollout / Migration
1) Implement contract in gateway (feature-flagged).
2) Update SDKs to surface typed exception and fields.
3) Update public docs and error catalog entry for `residency_policy_violation`.
4) Add conformance tests:
   - non-streaming block returns 409 + envelope
   - streaming block returns 409 pre-stream
   - request_id header/body match
5) After rollout, treat deviations (500s, missing fields) as P1 bug for residency-enabled orgs.

## Open questions (resolved)
- Expose policy identifier? **Yes**, as `policy_id` (opaque), optional.
- Which status code? **409 Conflict**.
- Streaming semantics? **Block pre-stream; return normal JSON error**.

title: SLO-aware dependency graph and release guardrails
preview:
Summary
-------
This decision documents a standardized approach for (1) annotating the runtime dependency graph with SLO risk metadata, and (2) enforcing release guardrails that adapt canary sizing, traffic routing, and automated rollback thresholds based on SLO risk tiers. The goal is to reduce blast radius from deployments and align rollout policy with customer-facing reliability targets.

Motivation
----------
We operate a set of loosely coupled inference and control-plane services with varying criticality to customer latency and correctness (e.g., routing, model-serving, kv-cache, billing). Historically releases have used a fixed canary ratio and static health checks; this mismatches business risk for high-SLO paths and increases manual review overhead. By embedding SLO metadata into the dependency graph and driving release policies from that metadata, we can: 
- Prioritize safety where customer-facing SLOs are tight (p95 latency < 150ms or error budget < 2% per week).
- Allow faster rollout for low-risk services (batching helpers, telemetry enrichment).
- Automate rollback decisions with deterministic, SLO-aligned thresholds.

Decision
--------
1) Each service in the dependency graph will be annotated with an SLO Risk Tier: CRITICAL, IMPORTANT, or LOW.
   - CRITICAL: Directly affects customer-facing latency or correctness (example: model inference runtime, tenant routing).
   - IMPORTANT: Affects internal throughput or observability that can secondarily impact customers (example: request aggregator, KV prefetch).
   - LOW: Non-customer-facing utilities or background jobs (example: metrics exporter).

2) Release guardrails are parameterized by tier. The following policy table is normative:

| Tier     | Canary initial ratio | Health signal window | Max failure tolerance (errors/s or p95 increase) | Forced manual approval? | Rollback action |
|----------|----------------------|----------------------|--------------------------------------------------|-------------------------|-----------------|
| CRITICAL | 1%                   | 10m                  | p95 increase > 25% OR error_rate > 0.5%         | Yes (SRE + owning ENG)  | Immediate rollback + circuit to previous model version |
| IMPORTANT| 5%                   | 15m                  | p95 increase > 40% OR error_rate > 1%           | No (auto monitored)     | Gradual rollback (reduce traffic by 50%) + alert |
| LOW      | 10%                  | 30m                  | p95 increase > 100% OR error_rate > 2%          | No                      | Continue with observation; manual rollback optional |

3) Dependency graph metadata fields (example JSON snippet):

```
{
  "service": "inference-router",
  "owner": "eng-serving-runtime",
  "slo_tier": "CRITICAL",
  "slo_targets": {
    "p95_latency_ms": "150",
    "error_rate_percent_weekly": "1"
  },
  "upstream_dependencies": ["model-executor", "kv-cache"]
}
```

4) The orchestrator (release service) will evaluate an aggregated SLO impact score for any release by walking the dependency graph from the changed service and computing the highest tier encountered. The highest tier determines the guardrail policy for that rollout.

Implementation details
----------------------
- Data model: The dependency graph will live in a single source-of-truth stored in config-repo (YAML) with an automated linter that enforces required fields (service, owner, slo_tier, slo_targets, contact).
- Linter rules (examples):
  1. slo_tier must be one of [CRITICAL, IMPORTANT, LOW].
  2. If slo_tier == CRITICAL, slo_targets must include p95_latency_ms and error_rate_percent_weekly.
  3. owner must map to a valid team in the org directory.
- Orchestrator integration: The release orchestration pipeline will call the graph service API to compute rollout policy. Example curl: 

  curl -X POST https://releases.internal/v1/plan -H 'Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN' -d '{"changes": ["service-a:v2"]}'

- Monitoring signals (PromQL examples):
  - p95 latency (service): histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{service=~"%s"}[5m])) by (le))
  - error rate (service): sum(rate(http_requests_total{service=~"%s",status=~"5.."}[5m])) / sum(rate(http_requests_total{service=~"%s"}[5m]))

- Health window and sampling: Guardrails use rolling windows as above; the release controller will evaluate online and require consecutive windows passing criteria for continued rollout.

Rollout and rollback procedure (runbook)
---------------------------------------
1. Pre-ship checks (automated):
   - Ensure dependency graph entry present and linted.
   - Verify canary image passes unit + integration suites.
   - Attach preflight perf test for CRITICAL services (short smoke perf run).
2. Start canary at configured initial ratio.
3. Monitor health signals for configured window.
4. If thresholds exceeded: 
   - CRITICAL: notify SRE & owning ENG, immediately execute rollback API (releases.internal/v1/rollback) and open incident if error budget breached.
   - IMPORTANT: automatically reduce traffic to 50% of canary, continue observation; if issues persist for 3 windows, rollback.
   - LOW: generate alert for owner but continue rollout by default.
5. When canary is stable for 3 successive windows, increment rollout to 25%, then 50%, then 100% with same checks.

Backwards compatibility and migration
-------------------------------------
- Existing services without slo_tier annotations default to IMPORTANT for two quarters. Teams must update their entries before the deadline, or they will be subject to manual gating.
- A migration tool (go binary: graphctl annotate) will be provided to scan runtime telemetry and recommend a starting tier for each service.

Ownership and governance
------------------------
- SLO taxonomy and tier definitions: eng-sre (primary), eng-platform (secondary).
- Dependency graph ownership: each service owner is responsible for maintaining their node.
- Release orchestration enforcement: eng-platform.
- Exceptions & appeals: service owners may request a tier variance via the reliability council; justification must include customer impact analysis.

Consequences and trade-offs
---------------------------
- Pro: Reduced customer incidents via targeted safety for high-risk paths; more developer velocity on low-risk components.
- Con: Additional overhead to maintain graph metadata and initial migration effort. Potential for conservative defaulting that slows some releases.

Related work
------------
- This ADR complements the existing traffic-tagging and cost allocation ADR (see related pages). It should be integrated with runtime feature gating and canary mesh rules.

Appendix: Example linter error output
-------------------------------------
```
ERR: inference-router.yaml: missing field: slo_targets.p95_latency_ms (required for CRITICAL)
ERR: feature-enricher.yaml: owner 'tools' not found in org directory
```

Appendix: Quick checklist for service owners
--------------------------------------------
- [ ] Add/validate dependency graph entry in config-repo.
- [ ] Confirm owner and oncall contact.
- [ ] Provide SLO targets if CRITICAL.
- [ ] Run graphctl annotate for tier recommendation.
- [ ] Schedule a brief post-deploy review for first three releases under new policy.

If you have questions or need help migrating, contact eng-sre@redwood-inference.internal or open a ticket in the Reliability board.
